text:
First thanks for all the work behind Subtext - it's been really inspiring!

I'll find the idea of incremental progress based on evaluation methodology generally agreeable. I definitely agree that 'coolness' isn't a great criteria. 

For criticism I have two points. First, science and research may not progress in clean incremental steps but in a somewhat haphazard ways - e.g. a big leap in the generally good direction may also include a bunch of crufty ideas. Then this could be followed by a series of simplifications to cut out the cruft from the useful ideas. Second, I'm not sure how an evaluation experiment would be designed to work around the 'familiarity bias'. It took me years to really get comfortable with the current (limited) forms of programming so there's likely a strong bias towards the familiar models. A significantly better but unfamiliar model may not appear to work as well in a limited evaluation experiment. Perhaps we need non-programmers to try these out?

First thanks for all the work behind Subtext - it's been really inspiring!

I'll find the idea of incremental progress based on evaluation methodology generally agreeable. I definitely agree that 'coolness' isn't a great criteria. 

For criticism I have two points. First, science and research may not progress in clean incremental steps but in a somewhat haphazard ways - e.g. a big leap in the generally good direction may also include a bunch of crufty ideas. Then this could be followed by a series of simplifications to cut out the cruft from the useful ideas. Second, I'm not sure how an evaluation experiment would be designed to work around the 'familiarity bias'. It took me years to really get comfortable with the current (limited) forms of programming so there's likely a strong bias towards the familiar models. A significantly better but unfamiliar model may not appear to work as well in a limited evaluation experiment. Perhaps we need non-programmers to try these out?

Talking about meta-problems, one thing that comes to mind is this: when we are creating 'new ways of programming' what exactly are we creating? Is it an underlying computation model (~lambda calculus/actors), a UI on top of an existing model, or something else? Usually it is all of the above - does it mean the factorization of the design space here is insufficient or imprecise? To make incremental progress as a group, maybe we need a little more structure - some kind of framework and/or language to describe what part of the problem a specific idea applies to and might help reason about how or if different ideas interconnect.

Talking about meta-problems, one thing that comes to mind is this: when we are creating 'new ways of programming' what exactly are we creating? Is it an underlying computation model (~lambda calculus/actors), a UI on top of an existing model, or something else? Usually it is all of the above - does it mean the factorization of the design space here is insufficient or imprecise? To make incremental progress as a group, maybe we need a little more structure - some kind of framework and/or language to describe what part of the problem a specific idea applies to and might help reason about how or if different ideas interconnect.

As experienced programmers we’re kind of stuck on this local optimum where we built ourselves a pretty comfortable environment around typing symbols into text editors. It’s hard to reach other, perhaps higher mountains around us without leaving behind even some of the stuff that we take for granted as crucial infrastructure.

One reason I think we’re stuck is because we usually take approaches that try to build a full-blown programming language that has all the expressiveness we’re used to from currently popular programming languages. I believe a new paradigm needs simplification, and that needs to get rid of (hide) some of the expressiveness.

Another reason I think we’re stuck is that we usually want to find a better way to program for people like us — other programmers. But as programmers we have so high requirements to what we want in a tool that it’s not feasible to target ourselves as the primary audience. My bet is on a future audience of people who don’t even want to learn programming today, who will be able to construct applications from simple building blocks to create or modify the applications they use today to change them to fit their specific need better.

I can see data scientists who learn programming today, because they reach the limitations of their tools. And while some of them learn to enjoy it, many just learn it to get stuff done they can’t get done otherwise.

I can see designers prototyping user interfaces, but then in the end instead of hiring a developer, they just push a few more buttons in their prototyping tool and will be able to export a proper application.

We need to meet these new audiences halfway, and abstract away some of the complexities we today believe are necessary to get anything done. Even if that means “it’s not really programming”. And that doesn’t mean our symbol-crunching compilers and interpreters will go away. Just like you can still drop down to assembly (or C, which is also “dropping down” for many these days), we will still be able to do that. Potentially to create the higher-level components our data scientist and designer friends will use to get their stuff done.

Do we need an explicit methodology for objective analysis? Ok, so coolness is out. So, what about the success of a system? Don't people say "Oh wow, this computer was built with Objective C and even though nobody else uses it there might be something to it?".

Do we need an explicit methodology for objective analysis? Ok, so coolness is out. So, what about the success of a system? Don't people say "Oh wow, this computer was built with Objective C and even though nobody else uses it there might be something to it?".

Do we need to rely on a sort of explicit advertising? Maybe we just create something successful with it and tell people how we did it. And yeah, ok, maybe creating something successful isn't so easy. But then again, if we could create rock solid systems in an order of magnitude less time couldn't we (at least some of the time) be more successful than people working with traditional techniques.

Do we need to rely on a sort of explicit advertising? Maybe we just create something successful with it and tell people how we did it. And yeah, ok, maybe creating something successful isn't so easy. But then again, if we could create rock solid systems in an order of magnitude less time couldn't we (at least some of the time) be more successful than people working with traditional techniques.

<@UAVRZE1UG> This reminds me of Alan Kay’s STEPS project. It wasn’t an order of magnitude less time, but 2-3 orders of magnitude less code (that should correlate somehow). So they’ve done this and achieved to build a fully working multimedia system (as they called it back then; OS + apps) in <20000 LOC, but somehow that didn’t take off or inspired other successful projects either.

<@UAVRZE1UG> This reminds me of Alan Kay’s STEPS project. It wasn’t an order of magnitude less time, but 2-3 orders of magnitude less code (that should correlate somehow). So they’ve done this and achieved to build a fully working multimedia system (as they called it back then; OS + apps) in <20000 LOC, but somehow that didn’t take off or inspired other successful projects either.

I've read the STEPS papers and wondered what ever happened to the authors? :stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye: But seriously, why did it stop? Did they run out of money and needed to get traditional jobs to pay the bills? If there are enough people pursuing this for long enough then someone has to eventually succeed, right?

I've read the STEPS papers and wondered what ever happened to the authors? :stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye: But seriously, why did it stop? Did they run out of money and needed to get traditional jobs to pay the bills? If there are enough people pursuing this for long enough then someone has to eventually succeed, right?

I think the STEPS project ended officially after the allotted time span and the people went to mostly Y-Combinator’s HARC and Bret Victor started the research that is now known as Dynamicland. Alan Kay shares his opinion about getting funding these days in pretty much every later presentation you can find of him. I think he has given up the hope that fundamental research is fundable in tech at this point. You either build a product and get Angel/VC money, or you’re done. I’d think he’d say such an endeavor is not feasible in a typical startup lifespan, as it needs more fundamental research.

I’d think he’d say such an endeavor is not feasible in a typical startup lifespan, as it needs more fundamental research.

I agree with Alan about the timescales of fundamental research vs start-ups, that's why I left that world and am attempting to self-fund my research. I'm still not sure how long this will last but I'm looking towards the amateur scientists of the 18 and 19th centuries as inspiration.

<@U5STGTB3J> Great points, and that is exactly the direction of my research. But even still it is too hard to do it alone and we need to find some way to work together. 

STEPS is cautionary tale: they tried to do everything on their own and put little effort into disseminating their results or seeking collaboration and engagement. So it had little impact.

+1 ... and if these values come with a way of testing for their presence (beyond I know it when I see it) then we have the sort of methodology I am calling for. But there is danger here too, for example the values of "expressive power" and "no boilerplate" can be quite controversial.

+1 ... and if these values come with a way of testing for their presence (beyond I know it when I see it) then we have the sort of methodology I am calling for. But there is danger here too, for example the values of "expressive power" and "no boilerplate" can be quite controversial.

I think the real value is in having a discussion about values. I don’t think we’re at a point yet, where we can just write down what we all believe in, as I’m sure we’ll discover some interesting differences. Great to see some momentum here!

<@UAVRZE1UG> Started reading the documents you posted. Very interesting! I agree to some parts, have some comments on others. Still need to read your three proposals, but very excited to learn more about what you’re working on.

In the meantime you might be interested in my “manifesto”, which I wrote a while ago, and which is about a broader set of things I think about. Gives you an idea where I am coming from: 
<https://github.com/stefanlesser/recurse/blob/master/manifesto2017.md>

text: Three thoughts: (1) finding a common set of values is a challenge in itself, (2) in finding our differences in values, we're likely to find the differing underlying contexts and challenges we face, (3) as <@U6KQ2S410> suggests vagueness is a great place to hide differences.

Three thoughts: (1) finding a common set of values is a challenge in itself, (2) in finding our differences in values, we're likely to find the differing underlying contexts and challenges we face, (3) as <@U6KQ2S410> suggests vagueness is a great place to hide differences.

It's great to see various 'manifestos' here. Based on this discussion I'm trying to extract the 'values' from various notes I have - though I'm not sure what qualifies as a 'value' (vs a design goal or principle). One value/aspiration I have is that 'programming should be tangible and live'. The broader theme perhaps is 'dont make me simulate the computer'. My post about that is here: <http://shalabh.com/programmable-systems/wheres-my-simulator.html|shalabh.com/programmable-systems/wheres-my-simulator.html>

text: <@U5STGTB3J> those questions seem good for reflecting on any values.
<@U5STGTB3J> those questions seem good for reflecting on any values.

<@U8A5MS6R1> Speaking of reflection, the kind of liveness you describe seems to emerge as online/interactive visualization/representation tools applied to a system capable of reflective awareness.  I just mean you can ask, "hey computer, what are you doing?"  In as much as the representation involves analysis and automated feedback, you end up with a sort of consciousness.  (Not to be confused with the phenomenological, first person subjective feeling of being consciously aware.)  I just want to point out that experience of live model has many parts.

<@U8A5MS6R1> Speaking of reflection, the kind of liveness you describe seems to emerge as online/interactive visualization/representation tools applied to a system capable of reflective awareness.  I just mean you can ask, "hey computer, what are you doing?"  In as much as the representation involves analysis and automated feedback, you end up with a sort of consciousness.  (Not to be confused with the phenomenological, first person subjective feeling of being consciously aware.)  I just want to point out that experience of live model has many parts.

And, friends, is it liveness itself that we value or is it liveness in the service of, say, comprehension?  I like liveness for its own sake,  feeling connected, in the moment, jacked in.  For comprehension, I recall a prodigious engineer capable of feely navigating swaths of Google – more like teleporting through it – armed with no more than a single large terminal window.  In as much as comprehension is our goal, practices to improve our own thought patterns may be the best path.

And, friends, is it liveness itself that we value or is it liveness in the service of, say, comprehension?  I like liveness for its own sake,  feeling connected, in the moment, jacked in.  For comprehension, I recall a prodigious engineer capable of feely navigating swaths of Google – more like teleporting through it – armed with no more than a single large terminal window.  In as much as comprehension is our goal, practices to improve our own thought patterns may be the best path.

I’ve put together a rough draft of what I consider the principles important to me for designing a tool that goes beyond current programming environments. Surely it’s not complete, and not phrased in the best way. 
Some of these principles will seem odd to you, and you might have different opinions about them. That’s where I‘m looking forward to discussion, not to tell you why I’m right and you’re wrong, but to find out together why we value different things, and how that can help us to learn from each other. We might discover that we want to build different tools for different domains and different audiences. That’s good; there is a lot of value in diversity. 
<https://gist.github.com/stefanlesser/ab0cc5a18baa37ad7b4cda63410ed554>

<@UA14TGLTC> I feel liveness/tangibility is important not just for comprehension but also things like immediate verification of ideas, easy exploration, accidental discovery etc. I agree that good thought patterns (mental models?) are useful as well, but wont a live visualization help greatly with *forming* good patterns (specially if the visualizations are well designed)? 

In the end I think mental simulation is just not scalable in a way that tangible, live visualization is. The debate between mental/internal and visual/external forms kind of seems like the old debate against writing via Socrates - the argument being that learning writing would 'introduce forgetfulness'. I believe even people with very strong mental capacity will benefit if they can use a more powerful external medium to help them.

<@UA14TGLTC> I feel liveness/tangibility is important not just for comprehension but also things like immediate verification of ideas, easy exploration, accidental discovery etc. I agree that good thought patterns (mental models?) are useful as well, but wont a live visualization help greatly with *forming* good patterns (specially if the visualizations are well designed)? 

In the end I think mental simulation is just not scalable in a way that tangible, live visualization is. The debate between mental/internal and visual/external forms kind of seems like the old debate against writing via Socrates - the argument being that learning writing would 'introduce forgetfulness'. I believe even people with very strong mental capacity will benefit if they can use a more powerful external medium to help them.

<@U8A5MS6R1> Accidental discovery is a good one.  Though we're in the midst of the liveness choir here, you are sure to find many outside who don't share our enthusiasm – often from those who found success despite poor visualizations.  (More than one of my university math professors felt diagrams were near useless.  Should have asked, "what's that all that over the chalkboard then?")  I suppose it's partly the curse of mastery: once you arrive you forget the scaffolding.  Add to that an aesthetic preference for graceful proof arches with all the motivating scaffolding removed.

<@U8A5MS6R1> Accidental discovery is a good one.  Though we're in the midst of the liveness choir here, you are sure to find many outside who don't share our enthusiasm – often from those who found success despite poor visualizations.  (More than one of my university math professors felt diagrams were near useless.  Should have asked, "what's that all that over the chalkboard then?")  I suppose it's partly the curse of mastery: once you arrive you forget the scaffolding.  Add to that an aesthetic preference for graceful proof arches with all the motivating scaffolding removed.

<@UA14TGLTC> 'many outside' also believe things are going spiffingly well in the programming world :smile:. Why must scaffolding be all removed in computers though? It's a medium that can show different views to suit the changing preferences of the reader. Have succinct, elegant proofs that are explorable, visualizable, interactive etc. with various scaffolding readily available.

Thinking more about it, I don't find it as simple as 'after mastery you dont need liveness/scaffolding'. Just like after mastery, we don't give up writing because it's a powerful tool that helps us do deeper/better thinking. I think 'liveness' is a fundamentally different mode of working and more powerful than just writing. It's a spectrum like [mental_only] < [mental + static_writing] < [mental + static_writing + live_interactive].

It's a medium that can show different views to suit the changing preferences of the reader. I think 'liveness' is a fundamentally different mode of working and more powerful than just writing. It's a spectrum like [mental_only] < [mental + static_writing] < [mental + static_writing + live_interactive].
